text: Black Rot is a fungal disease that attacks grape leaves, shoots, and berries. The most devastating symptom is berries turning completely black and shriveling into hard, mummified raisin-like balls that stay attached to the cluster. If left unmanaged in warm, wet weather, it can wipe out your entire harvest.

text: Common Rust is a widespread fungal disease of corn that produces small, powdery, brick-red pustules scattered across both surfaces of leaves. While it looks alarming, Common Rust is rarely devastating on field corn when resistant hybrids are used. However, on sweet corn and susceptible hybrids, it can cause significant defoliation and yield loss.

A: No - they are different diseases caused by different fungi. Southern Rust (Puccinia polysora) is more aggressive and damaging. Key difference: Common Rust pustules appear on BOTH leaf surfaces; Southern Rust pustules are mostly on the UPPER surface. Southern Rust pustules are also more orange-colored and smaller.

text: Grape Downy Mildew is one of the most destructive diseases of grapevines worldwide. It creates pale yellow "oil spots" on the upper surface of leaves, and a characteristic white, downy fungal growth on the underside. It attacks leaves, shoots, tendrils, and fruit clusters - a severe outbreak can destroy 100% of the crop and completely defoliate the vineyard within weeks. The disease is caused by a water mold (not a true fungus) that spreads explosively during warm, rainy weather.

text: Gray Leaf Spot is one of the most yield-limiting diseases of corn worldwide. It creates long, rectangular, gray-to-tan lesions that run parallel to leaf veins, eventually causing entire leaves to blight. The fungus thrives in warm, humid conditions with heavy dew - especially in fields with poor air circulation and corn residue left on the surface. Severe infections can wipe out 50% or more of yield by killing off the leaf canopy before grain fill is complete.

text: Potato Late Blight is the most dangerous potato disease on earth - it caused the Irish Famine and kills crops today. It creates large, dark, water-soaked spots on leaves with white cottony mold underneath, and causes reddish-brown rot inside tubers. It spreads at terrifying speed - an entire field can be destroyed in 1-2 weeks. Infected tubers spread the disease to storage, potentially destroying your entire stored harvest. Prevention is everything with this disease.

Q: I used metalaxyl and it didn't work - why?
A: Metalaxyl/mefenoxam resistance is extremely common worldwide. Switch to mandipropamid, fluopicolide, or dimethomorph immediately.

Q: How do I tell Septoria from Early Blight?
A: Septoria: MANY tiny spots (1-4mm), gray center, dark border, tiny BLACK DOTS inside. Early Blight: fewer but LARGER spots (5-15mm), concentric rings (bullseye pattern), yellow halo. No tiny black dots.
Q: The fruits look fine but leaves are falling off - should I worry?
A: Yes. Defoliation exposes fruits to direct sun causing sunscald (white/tan patches on fruit). It also weakens the plant's ability to fill fruit properly. Protect the upper canopy.

text: Spider mites are tiny arachnids (not insects) that you can barely see without magnification. They suck juice from leaf cells, creating a dusty, stippled appearance on leaves. In hot, dry weather they reproduce explosively - a single mite can become thousands in just 2 weeks. You'll know you have a serious problem when leaves look bronzed or silvery and you see fine webbing. They can kill plants if left unchecked in summer heat.

text: Spider mites are tiny arachnids (not insects) that you can barely see without magnification. They suck juice from leaf cells, creating a dusty, stippled appearance on leaves. In hot, dry weather they reproduce explosively - a single mite can become thousands in just 2 weeks. You'll know you have a serious problem when leaves look bronzed or silvery and you see fine webbing. They can kill plants if left unchecked in summer heat.
